patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] mm: add NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY node page counter
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new per-node page counter called NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY to track the number of dirty pages that have been dirtied via RWF_DONTCACHE writes, also known as dropbehind writes. The counter is incremented when a folio is dirtied with the dropbehind flag set and decremented when the flag is cleared or the folio is cleaned. This information will be visible in /proc/vmstat and used by the writeback flusher to determine how many pages to write back during expedited writeback for IOCB_DONTCACHE writes, without flushing all dirty pages.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/4] mm: kick writeback flusher for IOCB_DONTCACHE with targeted dirty tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses performance issues in the IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback path by moving writeback submission off the writer's hot path and into a background flusher. The patch introduces a new WB_start_dontcache bit that uses targeted dirty tracking to limit the scope of expedited writeback, preserving I/O batching while reducing latency. The patch also adds a new writeback reason for tracing visibility and targets the correct cgroup writeback domain. Performance results show significant improvements in sequential write throughput and latency, as well as random write throughput and latency.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Andrew Morton asked naive questions about the patch's implementation, specifically whether the WB_start_dontcache bit is properly cleared when a writeback flusher is kicked and whether the wb_check_start_dontcache() handler correctly handles the case where there are no dirty pages to write back.

Reviewer Andrew Morton questioned whether memory is properly cleaned up after generic_write_sync() returns, ensuring it's not still dirty or reclaimable.

The reviewer questioned whether the patch's change would prevent a buildup of dirty, unreclaimable pagecache, and asked if there is any mechanism to limit this buildup.
sentiment_signals: lack of clear objection or suggestion
review_comments.raw_body:
Naive questions...

On Sun, 26 Apr 2026 07:56:08 -0400 Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org> wrote:

---

So in the current case, when generic_write_sync() returns, all that
memory is written back and clean&reclaimable (or freed?), yes?

---

Whereas after this change, that pagecache is probably still dirty,
unreclaimable, waiting for the flusher to do its thing?

So is there potential that the system will get all gummed up with
dirty, to-be-written-soon pagecache?  Is there something which limits
this buildup?

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed Andrew Morton's concern about the IO submission timing in the writeback path, explaining that the current implementation submits I/Os before returning but doesn't wait for completion.

The author acknowledges that the current DONTCACHE I/Os do not wait for completion, but notes that this patch defers I/O submission to a flusher thread, which should eventually take care of business. The author does not indicate a need for further revision.

The author addressed Andrew Morton's concern about the patch potentially causing writers to be limited by traditional VM limits instead of the backing device throughput, explaining that this is actually a desired outcome and that the peak pagecache usage was higher but still less than with normal buffered I/O. The author suggests considering going back to submitting writes inline when the number of dirty pages is high, but notes that this could have negative performance effects.
review_comments.raw_body:
No. Before returning, it submits the I/Os for the portion that it wrote
rather than leaving it to the flusher to take care of things, but it
doesn't wait for the I/Os to complete.

---

Correct, but that's sort of the case today too since DONTCACHE I/Os
don't wait for the completion. With this change we're just deferring
the I/O submission to the flusher thread (which should hopefully soon
wake and take care of business). If the flusher thread can't keep up,
then eventually balance_dirty_pages() will kick in and start slowing
things down.

---

Today in this situation, the writers are limited by the backing device
throughput. Once the I/O submission queues are full, then the DONTCACHE
writers end up stacking up on those. With this change, the writers will
be more limited by traditional VM limits in this situation. 

In the test runs I did, the peak pagecache with DONTCACHE writes was
higher than with the unpatched version but still considerably less than
with normal buffered I/O. That's the cost of deferring the I/O
submission to the flusher.

One thing we could consider is going back to submitting the writes
inline when the number of dirty pages is high. But, that could have a
detrimental effect on performance too.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges that the mixed-mode benchmark results show decreased read throughput due to increased write throughput, and suggests there are multiple possible solutions to address this issue.

The author acknowledges that the patch introduces tradeoffs, but believes it's a win overall due to DONTCACHE being relatively new and having a small blast radius. They suggest considering kicking off writeback earlier in the normal buffered case as well.
review_comments.raw_body:
Forgot to comment on this part earlier...

This is the "mixed-mode" (dontcache writes + buffered reads). I played
with a bunch of different settings under nfsd, and those settings
turned out to perform the best with this benchmark.

I suspect what's happening is that the increase in write throughput
from writing via the flusher thread is crowding out reads. So, read
throughput suffers in this test from that. There are a number of ways
we could probably make that more fair.

---

I think it's a win overall. As with anything writeback-related, it's a
game of tradeoffs. The good news is that DONTCACHE is still fairly new
and not many applications are using it yet, so the blast radius from
any change here should be rather small.

As a side note: I've long thought that we in general wait too long to
kick off writeback with normal buffered I/O, particularly with modern
memory sizes. DONTCACHE gives us a place to experiment with this
scheme, but we may want to think about kicking off writeback earlier in
the normal buffered case too.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Matthew Wilcox expressed surprise that the patch's solution for 'noisy neighbour' problem doesn't address his initial expectation of concurrent DONTCACHE and buffered writes, suggesting a different scenario than what the patch aims to solve.
review_comments.raw_body:
hm.  This wasn't what I thought of when I thought of "noisy neighbour".
I'd have process A doing DONTCACHE writes to file A and process B doing
normal buffered writes to file B.

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the writeback flusher writes back all dirty pages, regardless of type, and suggested documenting this change in the commit message or man page to clarify the behavior for RWF_DONTCACHE writes.

Reviewer requested testing of parallel buffered and don't cache writes, noting that the patch series impacts this scenario.

Reviewer Ritesh (IBM) requested clarification on test scenario details, specifically pointing out inconsistencies in file size used for single-client tests versus multi-client tests, and suggested using fio's built-in aggregation capabilities instead of parsing results manually.

Reviewer Ritesh requested an explanation for the significant performance improvement (5x) observed on NFS compared to local filesystems, indicating that he is not familiar with the NFS side and would appreciate a clarification.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yup, so, we wakeup the writeback flusher, which will write those many
"number" of dirty pages. Those dirty pages written by writeback, can be
of any type though, can be DONTCACHE or normal (non-dontcache) dirty
pages. IIUC, writeback doesn't distinguish between them while writing.


IMO, what we could also include in the commit msg is why is this above
approach taken? IIUC, that is because, by writing NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY
pages, it still reduces the page cache pressure and still reduces the
amount of work that the reclaim has to do, even though some of those
pages maybe non-dontcache pages, in case if there was a parallel
buffered write in the system.


Also should the following change be documented somewhere? Like in Man
page maybe? i.e.
Earlier RWF_DONTCACHE writes made sure that those dirty pages are
immediately submitted for writeback and completion would release those
pages. But now, in certain cases when there is a mixed buffered write in
the system, those dontcache dirty pages might be written back after a
delay (whenever the next time writeback kicks in).
However for RWF_DONTCACHE reads, it should not affect anything.

---

Can we please also test parallel buffered writes and dontcache writes? 
Since this patch series definitely affects that.

BTW - adding these numbers in the commit msg itself is much helpful.

---

Can you please help describe this test scenario if possible.. In above
you mentioned we are writing file_size as 2x RAM_SIZE. But your
multi-client tests says something else..

local num_clients=4
+	mem_kb=$(awk '/MemTotal/ {print $2}' /proc/meminfo)
+	client_size="$(( mem_kb / 1024 / num_clients ))M"

Also the multi-writer case is spawning parallel fio jobs, and then
parsing and aggregating the bandwidth results instead of using fio to
spawn multiple parallel threads... which is ok, but a bit wierd.
Why not let fio do the aggregate bandwidth, and latency calculation
instead?

---

Nice :)
Some explaination here of why 5x improvement with NFS compared to local
filesystems please?
(I am not much aware of NFS side, but a possible reasoning would help)

-ritesh

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] testing: add nfsd-io-bench NFS server benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a benchmark suite to test the performance of NFSD I/O modes using fio and the libnfs backend against an NFS server on localhost. The suite includes tests for buffered, direct, and cached I/O modes via NFSD debugfs controls. It also includes scripts to orchestrate the test matrix, parse results from fio JSON output, and configure the NFS export for testing. This patch aims to provide a comprehensive benchmarking tool for evaluating NFSD performance in various scenarios.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton expressed concerns about maintainability of machine-generated code and requested that either the generation process be made accessible to other developers or the generated code be provided in a way that allows for regeneration.
review_comments.raw_body:
Seems that this code was largely machine-generated.  So I assume that
you're in possession of the scripts/prompts/whatever which were used to
generate this code.

(Can you please briefly describe the process which you used here?)

So how are we to maintain this?  Will other developers have to go in
and hack this machine-generated output by hand?  Or would it be better
to provide (in-tree) other developers with the means to regenerate this code,
presumably using Claude?

IOW, this feels a bit like shipping the .s file without giving us the .c
file!

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the patch generated benchmarks for DONTCACHE writes involving file sizes larger than machine memory, and confirmed that the tool did a 'reasonable job' of creating them.

Author is addressing Andrew Morton's concern about the inclusion of two benchmark suites, stating they are 'AI slop' and may not have long-term value or be maintainable.
sentiment_signals: questioned the value of the testcases
review_comments.raw_body:
It's been a while since it generated these, but I think I just asked it
to concoct a set of benchmarks for DONTCACHE writes when that involved
file sizes that were larger than the machine's memory. 

I ended up asking it to make some changes (e.g. the mixed-mode test,
and some of the perf stuff), but it seemed to do a reasonable job of
creating it.

---

As I mentioned in the cover letter, I mostly included this in the
series to demonstrate how this was tested. I'm not sure if the two
benchmark suites are suitable for inclusion. I'm fine with leaving
those two patches out of the merge. I found the testcases useful for
this, but they are indeed AI slop, and I'm not sure they have long-term
value or will be maintainable.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested moving the NFS server benchmark suite to xfstests, citing its existing performance testing framework and questioning whether maintaining separate fio-based scripts is necessary.
review_comments.raw_body:
Maybe in xfstests? It has tests/perf/, but that just have 1 test.
Maybe others can tell whether it make sense to maintain such fio based
performance benchmarking scripts in there.

-ritesh

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 4/4] testing: add dontcache-bench local filesystem benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new benchmark suite called 'dontcache-bench' to test the IOCB_DONTCACHE functionality on local filesystems using fio's io_uring engine and RWF_DONTCACHE flag. The suite includes various job files for different I/O patterns, such as sequential reads and writes, random reads and writes, and latency measurements. It also includes scripts to run and parse the benchmark results. This patch aims to provide a comprehensive testing framework for IOCB_DONTCACHE on local filesystems.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve write performance when using the RWF_DONTCACHE flag, which prevents caching of written data. The main change is a new scheme that kicks the flusher thread after a DONTCACHE write, causing it to perform writeback proportional to the amount written. This approach was suggested by Jan Kara and Christoph Hellwig during review of an earlier series. The patch also includes benchmark results in the last two patches, which show this method working as well or better than previous approaches.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Andrew Morton questioned the implementation of writeback after a DONTCACHE write, specifically pointing out that the flusher thread is kicked without checking if it's already running, which could lead to unnecessary wakeups.

The reviewer questioned whether memory written by generic_write_sync() is immediately written back and reclaimed or freed, requesting clarification on the writeback behavior.

Andrew Morton questioned whether the system could become congested with dirty, unreclaimable pagecache after a DONTCACHE write, and asked if there's a mechanism to prevent this buildup.
review_comments.raw_body:
Naive questions...

On Sun, 26 Apr 2026 07:56:08 -0400 Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org> wrote:

---

So in the current case, when generic_write_sync() returns, all that
memory is written back and clean&reclaimable (or freed?), yes?

---

Whereas after this change, that pagecache is probably still dirty,
unreclaimable, waiting for the flusher to do its thing?

So is there potential that the system will get all gummed up with
dirty, to-be-written-soon pagecache?  Is there something which limits
this buildup?

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton expressed concerns about maintainability and reproducibility of machine-generated code, questioning whether other developers would need to manually modify it or if regeneration tools should be provided.
review_comments.raw_body:
Seems that this code was largely machine-generated.  So I assume that
you're in possession of the scripts/prompts/whatever which were used to
generate this code.

(Can you please briefly describe the process which you used here?)

So how are we to maintain this?  Will other developers have to go in
and hack this machine-generated output by hand?  Or would it be better
to provide (in-tree) other developers with the means to regenerate this code,
presumably using Claude?

IOW, this feels a bit like shipping the .s file without giving us the .c
file!

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Jeff Layton addressed Andrew Morton's concern that the patch doesn't wait for IO completion after submitting writes, explaining that it instead leaves it to the flusher to handle.

Author acknowledged that DONTCACHE I/Os don't wait for completion, but explained that the patch defers I/O submission to the flusher thread, which should eventually wake and handle the writeback.

The author addressed Andrew Morton's concern about the patch's impact on write performance when I/O submission queues are full. The author explained that with this change, writers will be more limited by traditional VM limits in this situation and provided test run results showing a higher peak pagecache with DONTCACHE writes compared to the unpatched version but still less than normal buffered I/O.
review_comments.raw_body:
No. Before returning, it submits the I/Os for the portion that it wrote
rather than leaving it to the flusher to take care of things, but it
doesn't wait for the I/Os to complete.

---

Correct, but that's sort of the case today too since DONTCACHE I/Os
don't wait for the completion. With this change we're just deferring
the I/O submission to the flusher thread (which should hopefully soon
wake and take care of business). If the flusher thread can't keep up,
then eventually balance_dirty_pages() will kick in and start slowing
things down.

---

Today in this situation, the writers are limited by the backing device
throughput. Once the I/O submission queues are full, then the DONTCACHE
writers end up stacking up on those. With this change, the writers will
be more limited by traditional VM limits in this situation. 

In the test runs I did, the peak pagecache with DONTCACHE writes was
higher than with the unpatched version but still considerably less than
with normal buffered I/O. That's the cost of deferring the I/O
submission to the flusher.

One thing we could consider is going back to submitting the writes
inline when the number of dirty pages is high. But, that could have a
detrimental effect on performance too.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the benchmarks in patch #2 were generated for file sizes larger than machine memory, and confirmed they did a 'reasonable job' of creating them.

Author acknowledged that the benchmark suites may not be suitable for inclusion in the kernel tree, and is open to removing them from the merge.
review_comments.raw_body:
It's been a while since it generated these, but I think I just asked it
to concoct a set of benchmarks for DONTCACHE writes when that involved
file sizes that were larger than the machine's memory. 

I ended up asking it to make some changes (e.g. the mixed-mode test,
and some of the perf stuff), but it seemed to do a reasonable job of
creating it.

---

As I mentioned in the cover letter, I mostly included this in the
series to demonstrate how this was tested. I'm not sure if the two
benchmark suites are suitable for inclusion. I'm fine with leaving
those two patches out of the merge. I found the testcases useful for
this, but they are indeed AI slop, and I'm not sure they have long-term
value or will be maintainable.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author addressed Andrew Morton's concern about the 'mixed-mode' benchmark results, explaining that write throughput increases may be crowding out reads and suggesting multiple ways to improve fairness.

The author acknowledges that the patch introduces tradeoffs, but believes it's a win overall due to DONTCACHE being relatively new and not widely used yet. They suggest considering applying similar writeback strategies to normal buffered I/O as well.
review_comments.raw_body:
Forgot to comment on this part earlier...

This is the "mixed-mode" (dontcache writes + buffered reads). I played
with a bunch of different settings under nfsd, and those settings
turned out to perform the best with this benchmark.

I suspect what's happening is that the increase in write throughput
from writing via the flusher thread is crowding out reads. So, read
throughput suffers in this test from that. There are a number of ways
we could probably make that more fair.

---

I think it's a win overall. As with anything writeback-related, it's a
game of tradeoffs. The good news is that DONTCACHE is still fairly new
and not many applications are using it yet, so the blast radius from
any change here should be rather small.

As a side note: I've long thought that we in general wait too long to
kick off writeback with normal buffered I/O, particularly with modern
memory sizes. DONTCACHE gives us a place to experiment with this
scheme, but we may want to think about kicking off writeback earlier in
the normal buffered case too.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

The warning indicates that the lruvec state is not being updated correctly, potentially leading to issues with memory management.

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary: The reviewer, Matthew Wilcox, expressed concern that the patch's implementation of RWF_DONTCACHE does not align with their understanding of a 'noisy neighbour', which they envisioned as two processes writing to different files, one using DONTCACHE and the other using buffered writes. They did not provide specific technical details or suggestions for improvement.
review_comments.raw_body:
hm.  This wasn't what I thought of when I thought of "noisy neighbour".
I'd have process A doing DONTCACHE writes to file A and process B doing
normal buffered writes to file B.

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary:
Ritesh noted that the current implementation of writeback flusher does not distinguish between DONTCACHE and non-DONTCACHE dirty pages, potentially leading to delayed writeback for some pages, and suggested documenting this change in a man page.

Ritesh requested testing of parallel buffered writes and don't cache writes, noting that the patch series impacts this scenario.

The reviewer requested clarification on the test scenario described in the patch series, specifically pointing out discrepancies between the file size used in the description (2x RAM_SIZE) and the multi-client tests, which use a different calculation to determine client size. Additionally, they suggested simplifying the bandwidth aggregation by letting fio handle it instead of parsing and aggregating results manually.

Ritesh requested clarification on why the patch series shows a 5x improvement in write performance for NFS compared to local filesystems and asked for an explanation of this discrepancy.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yup, so, we wakeup the writeback flusher, which will write those many
"number" of dirty pages. Those dirty pages written by writeback, can be
of any type though, can be DONTCACHE or normal (non-dontcache) dirty
pages. IIUC, writeback doesn't distinguish between them while writing.


IMO, what we could also include in the commit msg is why is this above
approach taken? IIUC, that is because, by writing NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY
pages, it still reduces the page cache pressure and still reduces the
amount of work that the reclaim has to do, even though some of those
pages maybe non-dontcache pages, in case if there was a parallel
buffered write in the system.


Also should the following change be documented somewhere? Like in Man
page maybe? i.e.
Earlier RWF_DONTCACHE writes made sure that those dirty pages are
immediately submitted for writeback and completion would release those
pages. But now, in certain cases when there is a mixed buffered write in
the system, those dontcache dirty pages might be written back after a
delay (whenever the next time writeback kicks in).
However for RWF_DONTCACHE reads, it should not affect anything.

---

Can we please also test parallel buffered writes and dontcache writes? 
Since this patch series definitely affects that.

BTW - adding these numbers in the commit msg itself is much helpful.

---

Can you please help describe this test scenario if possible.. In above
you mentioned we are writing file_size as 2x RAM_SIZE. But your
multi-client tests says something else..

local num_clients=4
+	mem_kb=$(awk '/MemTotal/ {print $2}' /proc/meminfo)
+	client_size="$(( mem_kb / 1024 / num_clients ))M"

Also the multi-writer case is spawning parallel fio jobs, and then
parsing and aggregating the bandwidth results instead of using fio to
spawn multiple parallel threads... which is ok, but a bit wierd.
Why not let fio do the aggregate bandwidth, and latency calculation
instead?

---

Nice :)
Some explaination here of why 5x improvement with NFS compared to local
filesystems please?
(I am not much aware of NFS side, but a possible reasoning would help)

-ritesh

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Ritesh suggested moving the performance benchmarking scripts from the patch series into xfstests, citing that it has a tests/perf/ directory but only contains one test, and asked if others agree with maintaining such scripts in this location.
review_comments.raw_body:
Maybe in xfstests? It has tests/perf/, but that just have 1 test.
Maybe others can tell whether it make sense to maintain such fio based
performance benchmarking scripts in there.

-ritesh

patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch adds an NFS server benchmark suite, but its long-term value and maintainability are questioned.

review_comments.author: Ritesh Harjani (IBM)
review_comments.summary: Suggested moving the benchmark scripts to xfstests, citing its existing performance testing framework.
